text: Chapter 11 teaches improvisation over vamps and rhythm changes by introducing the riff as the primary melodic unit, defined as 'ideas put together and forced against the chord' that need not be diatonic (Definition of riff runs, Riffs over G major chords). Baker justifies riffs structurally — vamp changes move too fast for chord-tone runs (Why riffs replace chord runs in vamps) — and supplies a substitution rule licensing minor material over dominants (Substituting minor for dominant) plus a mandate that every run be picked with alternating up-and-down strokes (Up-and-down stroke mandate). The chapter installs Baker's load-bearing position vocabulary: position is named by the fret behind which the first finger falls (Defining position by first finger), each key has two governing positions (Two key positions per key) with C major as the lone one-position exception (C major position exception), and fixed positions anchor solo work the same way they anchor chords (Set positions for solo work). Baker's characteristic move surfaces in his internalization rule — riffs must be sung against the chords until they are heard before they are played (Internalize riffs by singing) — and in his pragmatic adaptation rule that dense runs should be thinned and slowed rather than abandoned (Simplify runs that are too dense). The chapter closes by defining 'rhythm changes' as functionally equivalent to vamps but conventionally in B-flat concert (Rhythm changes definition).
